claim: The manuscript uses a null-derived word-construction rule (e.g., a gallows-glyph prefix system) such that a small set of ~5 prefix glyphs accounts for > 60% of all word tokens, producing the observed Zipf exponent of ~0.89 via combinatorial explosion of a limited stem vocabulary
evidence_for: Word-initial glyphs are highly skewed: ['o','c','q','s','d'] dominate
Low Zipf exponent (0.8946) compared to natural language (~1.0â€“1.2) suggests constrained word formation
25 unique glyphs with average word length 5.03 is consistent with prefix+stem+suffix templating
evidence_against: Zipf R-squared of 0.9084 is a moderate fit, not exceptional â€” some natural languages also show exponents near 0.89
A prefix system alone would not explain the high hapax ratio of 70.1%
Glyph entropy 3.86 bits is close to Latin/Italian, suggesting phonemic rather than purely structural encoding
test: For each of the top-5 word-initial glyphs, compute what fraction of all word tokens begin with that glyph; then fit a prefix-stem bigram model and measure how much of the vocabulary is generated by combining those 5 initials with the top-50 word bodies
test_metric: Coverage fraction: (tokens with top-5 initial glyphs) / (total tokens); bigram generative coverage of vocabulary
test_threshold: Coverage > 0.60 and bigram model generating > 50% of unique word types would support a templated prefix system

tests_run.details: The hypothesis predicts frequent prefix-stem combinations, which should reduce hapax ratio; the observed 70.1% hapax ratio contradicts this mechanism. Low Zipf exponent (0.89) provides weak support, but high vocabulary diversity and uniform glyph distribution are inconsistent with 5 glyphs dominating 60% of tokens.

Glyph entropy of 3.8627 bits is inconsistent with hypothesis: if 5 glyphs dominated at 60% of tokens (12% each) while 20 glyphs shared 40%, entropy would be substantially lower (~2.8 bits); observed entropy suggests more uniform glyph distribution than null-prefix model predicts.

A null-derived prefix system with 5 glyphs accounting for >60% tokens should produce a steep Zipf exponent >1.5 through concentration; the observed 0.8946 indicates shallow uniform distribution contradicting prefix dominance, confirmed by the 70.1% hapax ratio showing vocabulary is highly diverse rather than concentrated in prefix-stem combinations.

The high hapax ratio (0.701) contradicts the prediction that a simple 5-prefix Ã— limited-stem combinatorial model would generate most vocabulary; such constrained systems produce much lower hapax rates through forced reuse.

Hypothesis is theoretically plausible as a null-derived prefix system could produce the observed Zipf exponent via combinatorial generation, but the relatively flat exponent of 0.89 and high hapax ratio lack strong support without explicit verification that the top-5 initials account for >60% of tokens and that prefix-stem combinations generate the observed vocabulary structure.

The predicted Zipf exponent (0.89) closely matches the observed (0.8946), strongly supporting the hypothesis. The high hapax ratio (0.701) is consistent with combinatorial prefix-stem explosion. However, glyph entropy of 3.8627 bits (83% of theoretical maximum for 25 glyphs) is higher than expected if only 5 prefix glyphs dominated >60% of tokens, creating partial uncertainty.

A 5-prefix Ã— 50-stem model generates max ~250 combinations, but the corpus exhibits 8,261 unique words with 70% hapax ratio; the vocabulary diversity far exceeds what simple prefix-stem concatenation could produce, contradicting the hypothesis.

Zipf exponent (0.8946) aligns with prediction, but hapax ratio of 0.701 contradicts the hypothesis that ~5 prefix glyphs with ~50 stems generate most vocabulary, since such recombination would produce far fewer unique single-occurrence words.

High glyph entropy (3.8627 bits, 79% of theoretical max) contradicts concentration in 5 glyphs; flat Zipf exponent (0.89) incompatible with skewed prefix dominance; vocabulary size (8,261 unique) far exceeds capacity of 5 Ã— 50 combinatorial model (~250 combinations).

The predicted Zipf exponent (0.89) matches the observed value (0.8946) extremely closely, indicating strong support for a prefix-stem combinatorial mechanism; however, the specified test for top-5 initial glyph frequencies was not computed from the provided statistics, preventing definitive confirmation.

Shallow Zipf exponent (0.89) and low type-token ratio (0.217) are consistent with combinatorial prefix-stem generation, but the large vocabulary (8,261 unique words) and high hapax ratio (0.701) are harder to explain with only 5 prefixes and 50 stems; without the specific prefix-frequency test results, evidence is inconclusive.
The 0.701 hapax ratio contradicts combinatorial explosion; 70% of vocabulary appears as singletons, not reused prefix-stem combinations.

High hapax ratio (70.1% of 8,261 unique words appear once) and 8,261 total unique words contradict the hypothesis: 5 prefixes Ã— 50 stems yields only ~250 combinations, insufficient to generate observed vocabulary diversity; while low Zipf exponent is compatible with the model, the combinatorial limit is fundamentally incompatible with the corpus statistics.
The 70.1% hapax ratio and flat Zipf exponent (0.89) contradict the predicted behavior of a concentrated 5-prefix system, which would generate high token repetition and a steeper rank-frequency curve.
High hapax ratio (0.701) and large unique vocabulary (8,261) strongly contradict a prefix+stem morphology; such a system would generate fewer unique forms with much lower hapax.

The high hapax ratio (0.701) and Zipf exponent (0.8946 with excellent RÂ²) strongly support a combinatorial prefix-stem model; non-uniform glyph entropy (3.86 vs max 4.64 bits) indicates concentrated prefix usage, consistent with ~5 glyphs dominating.

The flat Zipf exponent (0.89) and high hapax ratio (0.701) contradict the prefix-stem hypothesis; a 5-prefix system would produce steeper frequency concentration and fewer unique words relative to tokens.

High hapax ratio (0.701) and low Zipf exponent (0.8946) are consistent with combinatorial prefix-stem systems, but the moderate glyph entropy (3.86/4.64 max) and absence of the specified bigram model test results preclude definitive confirmation.

Could not evaluate: For each of the top-5 word-initial glyphs, compute what fraction of all word tokens begin with that glyph; then fit a prefix-stem bigram model and measure how much of the vocabulary is generated by combining those 5 initials with the top-50 word bodies
The 70% hapax ratio contradicts the hypothesis; a prefix-stem model predicts concentrated vocabulary reuse, but most words appear only once.

Zipf exponent (0.8946) matches hypothesis prediction (~0.89); high hapax ratio (0.701) supports combinatorial generation via limited prefixes; glyph entropy (3.86 bits) indicates non-uniform distribution consistent with concentrated prefix usage, though direct prefix-frequency measurement still needed for full validation

The Zipf exponent of 0.8946 is flatter than expected from a productive prefix-stem system, which should produce steeper rank-frequency decay when combining few prefixes with many stems; while the high hapax ratio (0.701) and low type/token ratio support limited vocabulary, the distribution statistics contradict the predicted frequency concentration in 5 initial glyphs.

Zipf exponent matches predicted value (0.8946 â‰ˆ 0.89), supporting the combinatorial mechanism hypothesis, but detailed prefix-stem bigram verification against top-5 initials and top-50 stems cannot be performed from aggregate statistics alone.

Could not evaluate: For each of the top-5 word-initial glyphs, compute what fraction of all word tokens begin with that glyph; then fit a prefix-stem bigram model and measure how much of the vocabulary is generated by combining those 5 initials with the top-50 word bodies

The 70.1% hapax ratio (most words appearing once) strongly contradicts a system dominated by 5 prefix glyphs + limited stems; such systems would show higher token repetition and lower hapax ratios. The unusually flat Zipf exponent (0.89) further suggests vocabulary diversity incompatible with combinatorial prefix-stem explosion.

reasoning: If word formation is template-driven rather than phonemic, decipherment attempts based on phoneme-to-glyph mapping will systematically fail; confirming this would redirect effort toward structural rather than substitution models
